speaker: interviewee
text: [BEEP]

speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is Wednesday, September 6, 1995. The survivor is Henry Silver; interviewer, Joyce Tapper; city/state, Los Angeles, California, United States of America; and the language of the interview is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm Joyce Tapper. I'll be interviewing Henry Silver here in Encino, California on September 6, 1995. Please begin, Mr. Silver.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Henry Silver. Before the war the name was Zilberszlak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your first name different, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first name was, Polish they call me Henryk. In Yiddish it was Hershel, or Horshel.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm born in the 7th of April, 1913, in the city of Radom, Poland. Radom was a town, about 120,000 people, included Jewish and Polish. Radom by itself was an industrial town. The Jewish people, especially myself, had very little to do in-- with the people, the Polish people. The reason was the Polish people in Radom, they had been very anti-Semitic. You couldn't go to Polish school, or Polish gymnasium, or college. The reason was, like I mentioned before--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you? Do you remember any anti-Semitic incidents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, was during my youth. There was lot a-- you can call this like that-- programs. Jews, people couldn't come to places where Poles used to go. This was the reason people, the Jewish people, concentrated themselves with themselves. I myself belonged to a Zionistic movement. And that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you get to that, please though, tell us a little-- go back a little bit and tell us about your grandparents and your family, things like that, before we learn about the Zionistic movement.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in Poland, families been very concentrated. I had grandparents.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather's name was [POLISH], [POLISH] Ryba, R-Y-B-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he do? What kind of work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not exactly like in-- families in Poland, they helped their parents, grandparents. Mine grandfather was more like a Jewish scholar, you can call him. He sit in beth hamidrash or the synagogue. He learned Torah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your grandmother? How many children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grandmother I don't remember because she died when I was very young, my grandmother. This I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children did they have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I remember-- what my mother told me here. She had only a sister what she was in Canada. I don't remember. After the war, I went to Canada to find it as about my family. And I finded my mother's sister. She got three, three sisters and a brother from a second marriage, for which, after the war, I went to Canada to find it as what has happened to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: One was-- the sister was Tila. I don't know how to spell it. Tila, Tila.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would their last name be?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Because I saw them a short time. It was a few days in Canada. The brother was Jechel. Jechel in Hebrew, I wouldn't know how to spell it. Oh, Jechel, let's say in Polish would be J-E-C-H-E-L, or something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your mother's family, some of them survived the war? This is your mother's family we're talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this-- if some of them survived the war-- I find it as mine aunt's son. He was a officer in the Russian army. I don't-- I never have a chance to meet him because we left Poland because I hate-- I got the feeling always, I-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE]. This is the reason, right in-- after I got back, my son-- we left. He hated Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand. Tell us now about your father's family, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Father's family-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I find nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war my father got a brother, with three nephews. The-- all of them was killed in the-- they was injected in the Polish army, and they all of them was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Now talk about what sort of life-- what was life like for you as a little boy in Radom? Tell us where you lived and what it was like.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I come from parents for this-- you can call this in, in Polish [POLISH] middle class. 90% Jewish was very poor. Was 10%, 15% were the-- could afford what you call this middle class. My father, we been in, they all of them been in business, china business. They most imported china from Czechoslovakia, from lot of factories in Poland, like, like Olkusz, [POLISH]. He tried to educate the, the children. We been-- 11 children.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many sisters, how many brothers out of the 11?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was six brothers and five sisters. Oh, some of them died very young.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were alive in 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1939, when the war start, it was alive-- how many boys and how many girls, you like to know? Was alive four boys and three girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many in 1945?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '45, after the war is, I finded two was hidden by Polish gentile people. And I myself went to concentration camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now describe your life. Pick a typical day when you were a school boy. What was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, a schoolboy like Jews, they couldn't participated in-- like here I'm now in the United States, blessed country. Everybody can go to school. Over there, the Jews, they had been very religious, especially the parents. They sent the children, they call this yeshiva or cheder.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to one?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to cheder. I didn't went to Polish school because, like I say before, we couldn't participated in life in general. Is we concentrate ourselves between youth movement, especially myself. I belong to a youth movement. I start when I was a youngster around 10 year old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it called?

speaker: interviewee
text: They called Hashomer Hatzair. This was our political youth movement what when you get 18 year old, you places to go to Israel. I started gradually when I was a kid [INAUDIBLE]. Every year I met the same kids until 18 years. It was by 18, and exactly 19 years old, I went to kibbutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the kibbutz?

speaker: interviewee
text: The kibbutz was in three places in Poland. [POLISH] The second was in [POLISH] The third one, [POLISH]. We was a group from many [INAUDIBLE] of very-- the same people what they met when they started. When they're young, they met every year in these camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in contact with any of these people after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Yeah. The organization was in contact, the people was in contact. We saw each, we saw each every year, almost every year. Some close to us, we used-- I used to go run with friends. We got bicycles. We went to run [INAUDIBLE]. We stopped run to see these people until-- I met him later, one when I was ready. They call this-- you have to be ready for-- to be-- make yourself productive to go to Israel. Because in Poland every Jew got the possibility to work, like United States, where he liked to work. You have to be shown where you work. It's an entire difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, were you in contact with any of these people following the war? The same group? Did any of them survive, is what I'm saying.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Some of them survived. I-- some of them survived, and I met them. We went to Israel quite a few time after the war. I met some of them and I met the kibbutz what is made-- you understand a little bit German? Anschluss, they make [NON-ENGLISH]. In Hebrew is, is with [NON-ENGLISH] because many died, many haven't survived the war. All of them met in Israel. And I met some of them came from, from Israel. They visit us here in the United States, in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now tell us what your house was like when you were a little boy. When you went to cheder, or yeshiva, excuse me, what was your home like? With all these children?

speaker: interviewee
text: The home like, my father-- the mother was help my father. And everyone participated in the business. Over there, if you had a business, it's not like here. United States, somebody else go in somebody's business. Over there, you got a business, from generation to generation is the same. And the kids take it over. Everybody's the same. The business was china.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the business in your same house, in the same building where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My so happened, my father was like, and I mentioned before, you could call this a middle class. He had his own house.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the house look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The looked house like, I would say, like the United States, a small apartment house with five, six apartments. And we got the most of the apartments, the children in the business.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was in the city, a city street?

speaker: interviewee
text: The city street? The city street was [POLISH]. We had the business on Rawinska. You want me to spell it in Polish? Doesn't make sense.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for holidays? What were they like in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, holiday was a big, big shindig in the, in the-- people was-- like was a holiday, you looked forward. Even when you was in [NON-ENGLISH] or [NON-ENGLISH] you looked forward for the holidays. Even you was the poor and the rich, was a holiday was very important life in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did many people come to your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Family used to come to the house in a holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like at a seder?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, seder was big. All of the children-- and the-- make big-- it's entire difference of people. Even this, people been very happy because it was family life. Entire difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: Paint us a picture. What was that table looking like, and who was sitting around it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Pesach was a holiday, a happy holiday. The father was the head of the table. The, the say the Haggadah, the history about the Yetziat Mitzrayim, the, the when people left Egypt, was translated and discussed. Because the period, the Jewish were slaves in Egypt. This is the whole story about Pesach.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the food good in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who cooked it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother cooked it. Very good, you reminisce even now. Tastes like mother's food. Even my wife, she cooks. She remembers-- mother was a good cook. Jewish, Jewish, cooking was very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember one dish that you looked forward to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything was good, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any holidays where you went away on a trip, like a vacation? Like a summer in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, if we went away, we always got in the cities where we went to visit, we got mishpacha, family. We went to visit them. And we participated in the same holiday with the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you close to your brothers and sisters? Were they your good friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of games did you play?

speaker: interviewee
text: What kind games? Well, it's a very important question you ask with games. It's-- if you compare with United States, United States people been entertained through television or, or, or professional teams. Over there, in Poland, each kid is two, three year old participated in games, everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your favorite?

speaker: interviewee
text: My favorite is figure skating, ice skating. I was three years-- let's see, I still live, was 79 year old. I was still skating. It's a shame-- I don't know. Skating got to do with more with, with balance. This is the, the reason what I was, I was a very-- you can-- I can call myself a professional skater. I even skate with professional skaters here, just two, three years ago, four years ago with professional skaters here in United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you skated was it outdoors? Outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wasn't in a rink?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where?

speaker: interviewer
text: In Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland? We had rinks, yeah, especially what I-- went skating not too many grownup used to go skating because the skating itself was expensive. Not every Jewish kid could afford it. The skates was expensive. And the entrance on the rink was expensive. And, and it was hard for Jewish kids. There was-- especially when you was a kid, from 11, 12 years. They called us-- here, I don't know how to say it in English. You've been beaten up. You had to fight them always. Because you Jewish, especially when you skate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there are many Jewish kids that skated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too many, like I say. I would say the class was I would say 10%, 15% what could afford it. Not all of them Jewish kids could afford it. And the parents couldn't afford it to, to buy skates. Excuse me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were  you-- did you make any friends among the Polish skaters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. The reason what I made friend is because I was a good skater. Is they made skate. I even organized a hockey team what I involved Polish kids because I haven't got enough good skaters that been able to compete in this particular game.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you played ice hockey?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, I played hockey. And I, and I was also a good-- you call this here soccer. Over there we call this football. This was a very important-- each kid participated. I also played in Jewish national teams. In soccer I always played-- bicycle was a sport. Every kid couldn't afford a bicycle. One in-- let's say one in 10, two in 10 could afford a bicycle. Very small group of people what they could.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what anti-Semitic incidents did you experience as a teenager yourself? What happened that you can remember to tell us?

speaker: interviewee
text: I myself was in a group, like I belonged to a youth movement what-- In the neighborhood-- same thing like United States. In United States it's like gang. Over there was not like gang. Was a difference because of anti-Semitic. They fight, and we were stronger, you know. And not, not everybody let beat himself up, beat himself up. I'm talking when we was kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe to me one event that you remember where you had a fight with somebody. Tell me about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it's ridiculous to tell it. We didn't try. We was-- kids would try to learn. We didn't learn in school. We learned like I-- I never want to repeat myself. We belonged to this youth movement. And we learned about everything, like you-- I mentioned to you. You like to know like we come back-- we will talk later on about this-- we come back for, for the Holocaust. I mentioned to you I was bitter. Nobody-- millions and millions been annihilated. And nobody even know until now 50 years later come a guy with the name Spielberg woke up the people and let them know, the people, what was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right now in our discussion, you're painting a picture for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: I give example.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I want you to show what led up to the big troubles for you. So that's why I asked you if you could remember, you know, a anti-Semitic incident or incidents that happened to you and your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: We lot of-- the whole time, lived under pressure when it come to Jews and Gentile, lot of pressure. The-- we, we didn't like the Poles. The Poles haven't liked us.

speaker: interviewer
text: You felt this every--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Poles, I think, it was a reason. They thought we, we haven't participated in their colleges and their schools, or intellectual, we'd been more advanced is a reason. A small percentage-- I find-- tried to find them a reason why we been hated because of religious-- like was a Gentile religion, a holiday, you couldn't come outside because, let's say, was the time when the holiday, the crucifixion when Jesus, the [INAUDIBLE] Jews. They crucified Jesus. This is the reason we, we tried to stay away, to educate ourselves, and to be always ahead like Jews-- I mentioned to you privately like they couldn't go to professional schools. Or this percentage, like 10%, 15%, what could afford it sent their children to Germany, to England, to France. And when they come back, they come back professional-- doctors, lawyers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of your brothers or sisters go away to school in foreign countries?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, we belong to-- our dream was to go to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you were ice skating, were you're competing against Polish youngsters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did that work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never start-- finish with a fight. We couldn't afford to win. I mean class, like A class, B class. Was many difference classes. If we would win, we would be beaten up. I'm talking about youngsters, kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever beaten up? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, many times, yeah, I beat up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me just one time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they chase you? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is-- the neighborhood was kids. Was a time we, we was ahead. Was time they was ahead. Oh, the parents, they didn't-- they raised the children a way they shouldn't get in fights. They shouldn't get-- because caused trouble between the parents. You know, they are always like this. We tried to stay away. Later on, in the later year, like--
